title: Closet Organizer from One Sheet of Plywood
summary: A full-height closet tower cut entirely from a single sheet of 3/4" plywood. Straight cuts, screw joinery, and a wall cleat to anchor it.
description:
One sheet of plywood, ripped into three long strips, becomes an entire closet organizer — two tall sides, a stack of shelves, and cleats to hang closet rods on either side of the tower. It's about the highest-value thing you can do with $50 of plywood and a Saturday.

The efficient move is to have the store rip your sheet into three 15-7/8"-wide strips before you leave — they're easier to haul, and you skip wrestling a full sheet across a saw. Everything after that is crosscutting those strips to length and screwing them together. If you want a furniture look instead of a shop look, iron edge banding onto the front edges to hide the plywood plies.

The part you don't skip is the wall cleat. A tall, shallow tower loaded with clothes is tippy; screwing the top cleat into a wall stud is what turns it from a hazard into built-in storage. If there's a baseboard in the way, notch the back of the tower around it with a jigsaw so the unit sits flat to the wall.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs plus edge banding if you add it

tools.note: Crosscuts the plywood strips to length; a track guides a clean cut.
For ripping the sheet into strips if you're not having the store do it.
Countersink bit for the shelf and cleat joints.
Optional — cleans up the front plywood edges.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped into three 15-7/8"-wide x 8 ft strips.

materials.name: Closet rods, cut to closet width
Wood screws, 3"
materials.note: Anchoring the cleat to wall studs.

steps.title: Rip and crosscut the plywood
steps.body: Have the store rip the sheet into three 8-ft strips at 15-7/8" wide, or rip them yourself on a table saw. Then follow the cut list, crosscutting the strips to length with a circular saw. If you want a finished front, iron edge banding onto the exposed front edges now, while the parts are loose and easy to handle.

steps.title: Assemble the fixed shelves
steps.body: Mark the shelf locations on the inside and outside of both sides with tape measure and square. Predrill and countersink, apply glue at each joint, and screw the shelves to the sides. Attach one cleat across the top inside, screwing through the outsides and the top to lock the box square — this same cleat becomes your wall anchor later.

steps.title: Add fixed or adjustable shelves
steps.body: Add the remaining shelves the same way as the first, or go adjustable with a shelf-pin jig and pins. If you use adjustable shelves, cut them 1/4" shorter so they drop in and out easily rather than binding.

steps.title: Anchor to the wall and hang the rods
steps.body: Find and mark the wall studs. Attach the wall cleats to the studs with a countersink bit and 3" screws. If there's a baseboard, jigsaw a notch out of the back so the tower sits flat. Then mount the closet rod sockets and hang the rods on either side of the tower.
materials: Closet rods, cut to closet width

images.alt: Full-height plywood closet organizer tower with shelves and side closet rods
